rawText:
We are on the edge of change comparable to the rise of human life on Earth.

Imagine taking a time machine back to 1750—a time when the world was in a permanent power outage, long-distance communication meant either yelling loudly or firing a cannon in the air, and all transportation ran on hay. When you get there, you retrieve a dude, bring him to 2015, and then walk him around and watch him react to everything. It's impossible for us to understand what it would be like for him to see shiny capsules racing by on a highway, talk to people who had been on the other side of the ocean earlier in the day, watch sports that were being played 1,000 miles away, hear a musical performance that happened 50 years ago, and play with my magical wizard rectangle that he could use to capture a real-life image or record a living moment, generate a map with a paranormal moving blue dot that shows him where he is, look at someone's face and chat with them even though they're on the other side of the country, and worlds of other inconceivable sorcery. This is all before you show him the internet or explain things like the International Space Station, the Large Hadron Collider, nuclear weapons, or general relativity.

This experience for him wouldn't be surprising or shocking or even mind-blowing—those words aren't big enough. He might actually die. But here's the interesting thing—if he then went back to 1750 and got jealous that we got to see his reaction and decided he wanted to try the same thing, he'd take the time machine and go back the same distance, get someone from around the year 1500, bring him to 1750, and show him everything. And the 1500 guy would be shocked by a lot of things—but he wouldn't die.

It would be far less of an insane experience for him, because while 1500 and 1750 were very different, they were much less different than 1750 to 2015. The 1500 guy would learn some mind-bending shit about space and physics, he'd be impressed with how committed Europe turned out to be with that new imperialism fad, and he'd have to do some major revisions of his world map conception. But watching everyday life go by in 1750—transportation, communication, etc.—definitely wouldn't make him die.

No, in order for the 1750 guy to have as much fun as we had with him, he'd have to go much farther back—maybe all the way back to about 12,000 BC, before the First Agricultural Revolution gave rise to the first cities and to the concept of civilization. If someone from a purely hunter-gatherer world—from a time when humans were, more or less, just another animal species—saw the vast human empires of 1750 with their towering churches, their ocean-crossing ships, their concept of being "inside," and their enormous mountain of collective, accumulated human knowledge and discovery—he'd likely die.

In order for someone to be transported into the future and die from the level of shock they'd experience, they have to go enough years ahead that a "die level of progress," or a Die Progress Unit (DPU) has been achieved. So a DPU took over 100,000 years in hunter-gatherer times, but at the post-Agricultural Revolution rate, it only took about 12,000 years. The post-Industrial Revolution world has moved so quickly that a 1750 person only needs to go forward a couple hundred years for a DPU to have happened.

This pattern—human progress moving quicker and quicker as time goes on—is what futurist Ray Kurzweil calls human history's Law of Accelerating Returns. This happens because more advanced societies have the ability to progress at a faster rate than less advanced societies—because they're more advanced. 19th century humanity knew more and had better technology than 15th century humanity, so it's no surprise that humanity made far more advances in the 19th century than in the 15th century—15th century humanity was no match for 19th century humanity.

So—advances are getting bigger and bigger and happening more and more quickly. This suggests some pretty intense things about our future, right? Kurzweil suggests that the progress of the entire 20th century would have been achieved in only 20 years at the rate of advancement in the year 2000—in other words, by 2000, the rate of progress was five times faster than the average rate of progress during the 20th century.

If Kurzweil and others who agree with him are correct, then we may be as blown away by 2030 as our 1750 guy was by 2015—i.e. the next DPU might only take a couple decades—and the world in 2050 might be so vastly different than today's world that we would barely recognize it.

overview.title: The AI Revolution: The Road to Superintelligence (Part 1)

sentences.original: We are on the edge of change comparable to the rise of human life on Earth.
sentences.translation: 우리는 지구상에 인류가 등장한 것에 비견될 만한 변화의 문턱에 서 있다.

grammarElements.text: comparable to the rise of human life on Earth
grammarElements.label: 형용사구 (change를 후치수식)

vocabulary.example: We are on the edge of a major breakthrough.
There is no comparable experience in human history.
The rise of the internet transformed society.

expressions.usage: 중대한 변화나 사건이 임박했음을 표현할 때
두 대상의 규모나 중요성을 비교할 때

patterns.pattern: We are on the edge of [noun] comparable to [noun]
patterns.explanation: 현재 상황이 역사적 대사건에 비견될 만큼 중대하다는 것을 표현하는 패턴.
examples: We are on the edge of a discovery comparable to the invention of fire.
We are on the edge of a crisis comparable to the Great Depression.

drillQuestions.question: We are on the _____ of change comparable to the rise of human life.
This change is _____ to the rise of human life on Earth.

sentences.notes: Vernor Vinge의 인용문으로 글이 시작된다. "on the edge of"는 "on the brink of"와 유사하지만, edge가 더 날카로운 전환점의 느낌을 준다. comparable to는 형용사구로 change를 후치수식한다.

koreanAlignment.en: We are on the edge of change
comparable to
the rise of human life on Earth

pronunciationNotes: comparable: /ˈkɑːmpərəbl/ — "컴퍼러블", 강세는 첫 음절. /kəmˈperəbl/이 아님에 주의
sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 인용(epigraph) — Vernor Vinge의 말을 인용하여 글의 핵심 주제를 권위 있게 제시

sentences.original: Imagine taking a time machine back to 1750—a time when the world was in a permanent power outage, long-distance communication meant either yelling loudly or firing a cannon in the air, and all transportation ran on hay.
sentences.translation: 1750년으로 타임머신을 타고 돌아간다고 상상해 보라. 세상이 영구적인 정전 상태에 있고, 장거리 통신이란 큰 소리로 외치거나 대포를 쏘는 것이었으며, 모든 교통수단이 건초로 운행되던 시대다.

grammarElements.text: taking a time machine back to 1750
grammarElements.label: 동명사구 (목적어)

grammarElements.text: a time when the world was in a permanent power outage
grammarElements.label: 동격 + 관계부사절 (1750을 설명)

grammarElements.text: long-distance communication meant either yelling loudly or firing a cannon in the air
grammarElements.label: 등위절 (either A or B)

grammarElements.text: and all transportation ran on hay
grammarElements.label: 등위절

vocabulary.example: The damage to the building was permanent.
A power outage left the city in darkness.
Long-distance relationships are challenging.
The soldiers fired the cannon.

expressions.usage: 독자를 사고 실험으로 끌어들이는 오프닝 장치
말이 끄는 교통수단을 유머러스하게 표현

patterns.pattern: Imagine [verb-ing] ... —a time when [description]
patterns.explanation: 사고 실험을 제시하며 대시(—)로 부연 설명을 이어가는 블로그 특유의 서술 패턴.
examples: Imagine living in ancient Rome—a time when gladiators fought in arenas.
Imagine visiting the moon—a place where gravity is one-sixth of Earth's.

drillQuestions.question: Imagine _____ a time machine back to 1750.
The world was in a permanent power _____.

sentences.notes: 이 문장은 Tim Urban 특유의 유머 스타일을 보여준다. "permanent power outage"로 전기 이전 시대를, "ran on hay"로 마차를 재치있게 묘사한다. either A or B 구문이 두 가지 원시적 통신 수단을 병렬로 나열한다.

koreanAlignment.en: Imagine taking a time machine back to 1750
a time when the world was in a permanent power outage
koreanAlignment.ko: 세상이 영구적인 정전 상태에 있던 시대

koreanAlignment.en: long-distance communication meant either yelling loudly or firing a cannon in the air
koreanAlignment.ko: 장거리 통신이란 큰 소리로 외치거나 대포를 쏘는 것이었으며

koreanAlignment.en: and all transportation ran on hay

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 사고 실험(thought experiment) — 독자를 가상의 시나리오에 초대하여 논점을 체험적으로 이해시킴

sentences.original: When you get there, you retrieve a dude, bring him to 2015, and then walk him around and watch him react to everything.
sentences.translation: 거기에 도착하면, 한 남자를 데리고 와서 2015년으로 데려온 다음, 돌아다니며 그가 모든 것에 반응하는 것을 구경한다.

grammarElements.text: and then walk him around and watch him react to everything
grammarElements.label: 병렬 동사 (walk + watch)

vocabulary.example: The dog retrieved the ball from the lake.
Some random dude walked up to me.
How did she react to the news?

expressions.usage: 누군가를 여기저기 구경시킬 때
지각동사 watch + 목적어 + 동사원형 구조

patterns.pattern: When you get there, you [verb A], [verb B], and then [verb C]
patterns.explanation: 시간 순서대로 행동을 나열하는 서사 패턴. 구어적이고 캐주얼한 톤.
examples: When you get there, you check in, drop off your bags, and then explore the city.
When you arrive, you sign in, grab a badge, and then find your seat.

drillQuestions.question: You _____ a dude from 1750 and bring him to 2015.
You walk him around and watch him _____ to everything.

sentences.notes: "dude"라는 속어를 사용하여 캐주얼한 톤을 유지한다. "watch him react"는 지각동사(watch) + 목적어 + 동사원형 구조로, "그가 반응하는 것을 보다"라는 의미. 이런 비격식적 톤이 Wait But Why 블로그의 특징.

koreanAlignment.en: When you get there
you retrieve a dude
bring him to 2015
and then walk him around
and watch him react to everything
koreanAlignment.ko: 그가 모든 것에 반응하는 것을 구경한다

sentences.original: It's impossible for us to understand what it would be like for him to see shiny capsules racing by on a highway, talk to people who had been on the other side of the ocean earlier in the day, watch sports that were being played 1,000 miles away, hear a musical performance that happened 50 years ago, and play with my magical wizard rectangle that he could use to capture a real-life image or record a living moment, generate a map with a paranormal moving blue dot that shows him where he is, look at someone's face and chat with them even though they're on the other side of the country, and worlds of other inconceivable sorcery.
sentences.translation: 번쩍이는 캡슐이 고속도로를 질주하고, 그날 아침 대양 건너편에 있던 사람들과 대화하고, 1,000마일 떨어진 곳에서 진행 중인 스포츠를 보고, 50년 전에 있었던 음악 공연을 듣고, 실제 이미지를 포착하거나 살아 있는 순간을 기록하고, 초자연적으로 움직이는 파란 점이 있는 지도를 생성하고, 나라 반대편에 있는 사람의 얼굴을 보며 대화하는 등 온갖 상상할 수 없는 마법을 구사하는 나의 마법사 직사각형으로 놀 수 있다는 것이 그에게 어떤 경험일지 우리로서는 이해할 수가 없다.

grammarElements.text: It's impossible for us to understand
grammarElements.label: 가주어(It) + 진주어(to understand)

grammarElements.text: what it would be like for him
grammarElements.label: 의문사절 (what — understand의 목적어)

grammarElements.text: to see shiny capsules racing by ...
grammarElements.label: to부정사 (what it would be like의 내용)

grammarElements.text: and play with my magical wizard rectangle
grammarElements.label: 병렬 동사 (see, talk, watch, hear, play)

grammarElements.text: that he could use to capture ...
grammarElements.label: 관계대명사절 (that — rectangle을 수식)

vocabulary.example: The space capsule returned to Earth safely.
The movie was about paranormal activity.
The scale of the universe is almost inconceivable.
To ancient people, electricity would seem like sorcery.

expressions.phrase: it's impossible for us to understand
expressions.usage: 가주어 It + for + 의미상 주어 + to부정사 구조
가상의 경험이 어떨지를 묘사할 때
1750년 사람의 관점에서 현대 기술을 표현한 유머

patterns.pattern: It's impossible for [someone] to understand what it would be like to [verb]
patterns.explanation: 가상의 경험이 상상을 초월한다는 것을 표현하는 구문. 가주어 It + to부정사 진주어.
examples: It's impossible for us to understand what it would be like to live without gravity.
It's impossible for modern people to understand what it would be like to have no electricity.

drillQuestions.question: It's impossible _____ us to understand what it would be like.
He could play with worlds of other inconceivable _____.

sentences.notes: 이 문장은 글 전체에서 가장 긴 문장 중 하나. "magical wizard rectangle"은 스마트폰을 1750년 사람의 관점에서 묘사한 것으로, Tim Urban의 시그니처 유머다. 5개의 병렬 동사(see, talk, watch, hear, play)가 현대 기술의 다양함을 나열한다.

koreanAlignment.en: It's impossible for us to understand
what it would be like for him
to see shiny capsules racing by on a highway
koreanAlignment.ko: 번쩍이는 캡슐이 고속도로를 질주하는 것을 보고

koreanAlignment.en: and play with my magical wizard rectangle
and worlds of other inconceivable sorcery

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 낯설게 하기(defamiliarization) — 현대 기술을 1750년 사람의 관점에서 "마법"으로 묘사하여 독자에게 기술 발전의 크기를 체감시킴

sentences.original: This is all before you show him the internet or explain things like the International Space Station, the Large Hadron Collider, nuclear weapons, or general relativity.
sentences.translation: 이 모든 것이 인터넷을 보여주거나, 국제우주정거장, 대형 강입자 충돌기, 핵무기, 또는 일반상대성이론 같은 것들을 설명해 주기도 전이다.

grammarElements.text: all before you show him the internet
grammarElements.label: 보어 (before 종속절 포함)

grammarElements.text: or explain things like the International Space Station, the Large Hadron Collider, nuclear weapons, or general relativity
grammarElements.label: 등위절 (or + 나열)

vocabulary.example: Einstein published his theory of general relativity in 1915.
The particle collider accelerates particles to near light speed.

expressions.usage: 이미 나열한 것보다 더 놀라운 것이 남아 있음을 강조
예시를 나열할 때 사용하는 캐주얼한 표현

patterns.pattern: This is all before you [verb] ... or explain things like [A], [B], [C], or [D]
patterns.explanation: 이미 충분히 놀라운 것을 제시한 뒤, 아직 더 놀라운 것이 남았다고 강조하는 클라이맥스 패턴.
examples: This is all before you mention the smartphone or explain things like social media, GPS, or streaming.
This is all before you factor in things like inflation, taxes, or insurance.

drillQuestions.question: This is all _____ you show him the internet.
You explain things _____ the International Space Station.

sentences.notes: "This is all before"는 강력한 수사적 장치다. 앞에서 엄청난 양의 놀라움을 나열한 뒤, "이건 아직 시작도 안 한 거다"라고 말하는 효과. 네 가지 예시(ISS, LHC, 핵무기, 상대성이론)가 과학의 다양한 분야를 대표한다.

koreanAlignment.en: This is all before
you show him the internet
or explain things like
the International Space Station, the Large Hadron Collider, nuclear weapons, or general relativity
koreanAlignment.ko: 국제우주정거장, 대형 강입자 충돌기, 핵무기, 또는 일반상대성이론

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 점층법(climax) — 이미 놀라운 나열 뒤에 더 놀라운 것이 남았다고 하여 임팩트를 극대화

sentences.original: This experience for him wouldn't be surprising or shocking or even mind-blowing—those words aren't big enough.
sentences.translation: 그에게 이 경험은 놀랍거나, 충격적이거나, 심지어 정신이 나갈 정도가 아닐 것이다—그 단어들로는 충분하지 않다.

grammarElements.text: surprising or shocking or even mind-blowing
grammarElements.label: 보어 (3개 형용사 병렬)

vocabulary.example: The special effects in the movie were mind-blowing.
The news was absolutely shocking.

expressions.usage: 경험의 강도가 기존 어휘로 표현할 수 없을 만큼 크다는 것을 강조
점층적 나열의 마지막에 가장 강한 표현을 배치할 때

patterns.pattern: [Experience] wouldn't be [A] or [B] or even [C]—those words aren't big enough.
patterns.explanation: 형용사를 점층적으로 나열한 뒤 모두 부족하다고 부정하여, 경험의 규모를 역설적으로 강조하는 패턴.
examples: The view wouldn't be pretty or beautiful or even breathtaking—those words aren't big enough.
The discovery wouldn't be interesting or exciting or even revolutionary—those words aren't big enough.

drillQuestions.question: This experience wouldn't be surprising or shocking or even _____.
Those words _____ big enough to describe the experience.

sentences.notes: surprising → shocking → mind-blowing으로 강도가 점점 올라가는 점층법(climax). 그런데 이 세 단어조차 부족하다고 말하여 역설적으로 경험의 크기를 표현한다. 대시(—) 뒤의 "those words aren't big enough"가 펀치라인 역할.

koreanAlignment.en: This experience for him
wouldn't be surprising or shocking or even mind-blowing
koreanAlignment.ko: 놀랍거나, 충격적이거나, 심지어 정신이 나갈 정도가 아닐 것이다

koreanAlignment.en: those words aren't big enough

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 부정을 통한 강조(litotes/negation) + 점층법(climax) — 형용사를 나열한 뒤 모두 부족하다고 부정하여 경험을 표현 불가능한 수준으로 격상

sentences.original: He might actually die.
sentences.translation: 그는 실제로 죽을지도 모른다.

vocabulary.example: I actually enjoyed the lecture.

expressions.usage: 과장이 아니라 진짜 가능성이 있음을 강조

patterns.pattern: [Subject] might actually [verb].
patterns.explanation: 짧고 충격적인 한 문장으로 앞의 긴 설명을 극적으로 마무리하는 패턴.
examples: She might actually faint from the surprise.
This technology might actually change everything.

drillQuestions.question: He _____ actually die from the shock.

sentences.notes: 의도적으로 극도로 짧은 문장. 앞의 매우 긴 문장들 뒤에 배치되어 극적인 대비 효과를 만든다. "die"는 문자 그대로의 의미와 과장법(hyperbole) 사이를 오가며, 글의 유머와 심각함을 동시에 전달한다.

koreanAlignment.en: He might actually die

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 과장법(hyperbole) + 짧은 문장 효과(brevity) — 긴 나열 뒤의 단 네 단어로 극적 임팩트 전달

sentences.original: But here's the interesting thing—if he then went back to 1750 and got jealous that we got to see his reaction and decided he wanted to try the same thing, he'd take the time machine and go back the same distance, get someone from around the year 1500, bring him to 1750, and show him everything.
sentences.translation: 그런데 흥미로운 것은—그가 1750년으로 돌아가서, 우리가 자기 반응을 보는 재미를 누린 것이 부러워져 같은 것을 해 보고 싶다고 결심했다면, 타임머신을 타고 같은 거리만큼 과거로 가서, 1500년쯤에서 누군가를 데려와 1750년을 보여줄 것이다.

grammarElements.text: if he then went back to 1750
grammarElements.label: 조건절 (if — 가정법 과거)

grammarElements.text: and got jealous that we got to see his reaction
grammarElements.label: 등위절 (got jealous + that절)

grammarElements.text: and decided he wanted to try the same thing
grammarElements.label: 등위절 (decided + 명사절)

grammarElements.text: he'd take the time machine and go back the same distance
grammarElements.label: 주절 (결과 — 가정법)

grammarElements.text: get someone from around the year 1500, bring him to 1750, and show him everything
grammarElements.label: 병렬 동사 (get, bring, show)

vocabulary.example: She got jealous of her friend's success.
His reaction to the news was priceless.
They traveled the same distance in half the time.

expressions.usage: 새로운 반전이나 통찰을 소개할 때 사용하는 담화 전환 표현
get to + 동사원형으로 "~할 기회/행운을 얻다"

patterns.pattern: But here's the interesting thing—if [condition], [result]
patterns.explanation: 논증의 전환점에서 새로운 관점이나 반전을 소개하는 패턴. 블로그와 강연에서 자주 사용.
examples: But here's the interesting thing—if you reverse the experiment, the results are completely different.
But here's the interesting thing—if history had gone differently, we might not be here.

drillQuestions.question: But here's the _____ thing—the 1500 guy wouldn't die.
He got _____ that we got to see his reaction.

sentences.notes: "got jealous that we got to see"에서 got이 두 번 다른 의미로 사용된다: (1) got jealous = became jealous, (2) got to see = had the opportunity to see. 전체 문장은 가정법 과거(went/got/decided → he'd take)로 비현실적 가정을 표현.

koreanAlignment.en: here's the interesting thing
if he then went back to 1750
got jealous that we got to see his reaction
koreanAlignment.ko: 우리가 자기 반응을 본 것이 부러워져

koreanAlignment.en: he'd take the time machine and go back the same distance
koreanAlignment.ko: 타임머신을 타고 같은 거리만큼 과거로 가서

koreanAlignment.en: get someone from around the year 1500
bring him to 1750, and show him everything

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 사고 실험 확장(extended thought experiment) — 원래의 시나리오를 한 단계 더 진행시켜 논점을 심화

sentences.original: And the 1500 guy would be shocked by a lot of things—but he wouldn't die.
sentences.translation: 1500년의 그 남자는 많은 것에 충격을 받겠지만—죽지는 않을 것이다.

vocabulary.example: Everyone was shocked by the announcement.

expressions.phrase: would be shocked by ... but wouldn't die
expressions.usage: 앞선 극단적 결과와 대비하여 차이를 부각

patterns.pattern: [Subject] would be [adjective] by [X]—but [subject] wouldn't [verb].
patterns.explanation: 대시와 but을 사용하여 기대와 결과의 차이를 극적으로 대비하는 패턴.
examples: She would be impressed by the changes—but she wouldn't be amazed.
They would be surprised by the technology—but they wouldn't be overwhelmed.

drillQuestions.question: The 1500 guy would be _____ by a lot of things.
He wouldn't _____ like the 1750 guy would.

sentences.notes: "but he wouldn't die"가 문장의 핵심 펀치라인. 앞서 1750년 사람이 "might actually die"했던 것과 직접 대비되며, 이 차이가 논증의 핵심 근거가 된다. 대시(—)가 극적 전환점 역할.

koreanAlignment.en: the 1500 guy would be shocked
by a lot of things
but he wouldn't die

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 대비(contrast) — "shocked but wouldn't die"로 1750→2015 점프와의 극적 차이를 부각

sentences.original: It would be far less of an insane experience for him, because while 1500 and 1750 were very different, they were much less different than 1750 to 2015.
sentences.translation: 그에게는 훨씬 덜 미친 듯한 경험이 될 것이다. 1500년과 1750년이 매우 달랐지만, 1750년에서 2015년까지의 차이보다는 훨씬 적었기 때문이다.

grammarElements.text: far less of an insane experience
grammarElements.label: 보어 (비교급 less + 명사구)

grammarElements.text: because while 1500 and 1750 were very different
grammarElements.label: 양보절 (while = although)

grammarElements.text: they were much less different than 1750 to 2015
grammarElements.label: 주절 (비교급)

vocabulary.example: The pace of change has been insane.
It costs far less than expected.

expressions.usage: far가 비교급 less를 강조하여 차이의 크기를 부각

expressions.phrase: while ... were very different, they were much less different than
expressions.usage: 양보와 비교를 결합한 복합 논증 구조

patterns.pattern: While [A] and [B] were very different, they were much less different than [B] to [C]
patterns.explanation: 세 시점을 비교하여 변화의 가속을 논증하는 핵심 패턴.
examples: While 2000 and 2010 were very different, they were much less different than 2010 to 2020.
While the 1960s and 1980s were very different, they were much less different than the 1990s to today.

drillQuestions.question: It would be far _____ of an insane experience.
1500 and 1750 were very different, but much _____ different than 1750 to 2015.

sentences.notes: while이 여기서는 "~하는 동안"이 아니라 "~이긴 하지만"이라는 양보의 의미로 사용된다(= although). "less different"라는 비교급이 글의 핵심 논증—변화 속도의 가속—을 직접적으로 뒷받침한다.

koreanAlignment.en: It would be far less of an insane experience
koreanAlignment.ko: 훨씬 덜 미친 듯한 경험이 될 것이다

koreanAlignment.en: because while 1500 and 1750 were very different
they were much less different than 1750 to 2015

sentences.original: The 1500 guy would learn some mind-bending shit about space and physics, he'd be impressed with how committed Europe turned out to be with that new imperialism fad, and he'd have to do some major revisions of his world map conception.
sentences.translation: 1500년의 그 남자는 우주와 물리학에 대한 정신이 휘어질 만한 이야기를 듣게 될 것이고, 유럽이 그 새로운 제국주의 유행에 얼마나 진심이었는지 감명을 받을 것이며, 자기 세계 지도 개념을 대폭 수정해야 할 것이다.

grammarElements.text: some mind-bending shit about space and physics
grammarElements.label: 목적어

grammarElements.text: he'd be impressed with how committed Europe turned out to be
grammarElements.label: 등위절 (impressed + 감탄사절)

grammarElements.text: he'd have to do some major revisions of his world map conception
grammarElements.label: 등위절

vocabulary.example: Quantum physics is truly mind-bending.
European imperialism reshaped the world map.
Many people thought the internet was just a fad.
His conception of the world was outdated.

expressions.usage: 예상과 다른 결과를 표현할 때
큰 변경이 필요할 때

patterns.pattern: [Subject] would [verb A], he'd be [adjective] with [X], and he'd have to [verb B]
patterns.explanation: 가정법으로 세 가지 병렬 결과를 나열하는 서술 패턴.
examples: She would discover new music, she'd be impressed with the art scene, and she'd have to update her preferences.
He would notice the changes, he'd be surprised by the growth, and he'd have to rethink his assumptions.

drillQuestions.question: He'd be _____ with how committed Europe was to imperialism.
Imperialism was described as a new _____.

sentences.notes: 제국주의를 "fad(유행)"라고 부르는 것이 Tim Urban의 날카로운 아이러니. 수백 년간 세계를 지배한 제국주의를 가벼운 유행어로 치환하여 1500년 사람의 관점을 유머러스하게 표현한다. "mind-bending shit"는 매우 비격식적 표현.

koreanAlignment.en: The 1500 guy would learn
some mind-bending shit about space and physics
koreanAlignment.ko: 우주와 물리학에 대한 정신이 휘어질 만한 이야기를

koreanAlignment.en: he'd be impressed with how committed Europe turned out to be
koreanAlignment.ko: 유럽이 얼마나 진심이었는지 감명을 받을 것이며

koreanAlignment.en: with that new imperialism fad
he'd have to do some major revisions of his world map conception
koreanAlignment.ko: 세계 지도 개념을 대폭 수정해야 할 것이다

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 아이러니(irony) — 제국주의를 "fad(유행)"라고 칭하여 역사적 사건을 1500년 관점에서 희화화

sentences.original: But watching everyday life go by in 1750—transportation, communication, etc.—definitely wouldn't make him die.
sentences.translation: 하지만 1750년의 일상생활—교통, 통신 등—을 바라보는 것은 절대 그를 죽게 만들지는 않을 것이다.

grammarElements.text: watching everyday life go by in 1750
grammarElements.label: 동명사구 (주어)

vocabulary.example: Technology has changed our everyday life.
This is definitely the right decision.

expressions.usage: 지각동사 watch + 목적어 + go by 구조
사역동사 make를 사용한 인과 표현

patterns.pattern: But [verb-ing X]—[examples]—definitely wouldn't make [someone] [verb]
patterns.explanation: 대시 사이에 예시를 삽입하며, 부정 강조로 결론을 내리는 패턴.
examples: But watching modern technology—smartphones, laptops, etc.—definitely wouldn't confuse a 1990s person.
But learning basic concepts—addition, subtraction, etc.—definitely wouldn't overwhelm the students.

drillQuestions.question: Watching everyday life go by _____ wouldn't make him die.
It wouldn't _____ him die.

sentences.notes: 사역동사 make + 목적어 + 동사원형 구조: "make him die" (그를 죽게 만들다). "die"가 이 글에서 핵심 척도로 반복 사용된다—충격의 정도가 "죽을 만큼"인지 아닌지가 논증의 기준.

koreanAlignment.en: But watching everyday life go by in 1750
transportation, communication, etc.
definitely wouldn't make him die
koreanAlignment.ko: 절대 그를 죽게 만들지는 않을 것이다

sentences.original: No, in order for the 1750 guy to have as much fun as we had with him, he'd have to go much farther back—maybe all the way back to about 12,000 BC, before the First Agricultural Revolution gave rise to the first cities and to the concept of civilization.
sentences.translation: 아니, 1750년의 그 남자가 우리가 그에게서 느낀 만큼의 재미를 얻으려면, 훨씬 더 먼 과거로 가야 한다—아마 기원전 약 12,000년까지, 최초의 농업 혁명이 최초의 도시와 문명이라는 개념을 탄생시키기 전으로.

grammarElements.text: in order for the 1750 guy to have as much fun as we had with him
grammarElements.label: 목적절 (in order for + 의미상 주어 + to부정사)

grammarElements.text: he'd have to go much farther back
grammarElements.label: 주절 (의무/필요)

grammarElements.text: maybe all the way back to about 12,000 BC
grammarElements.label: 부사구 (구체적 시점)

grammarElements.text: before the First Agricultural Revolution gave rise to the first cities and to the concept of civilization
grammarElements.label: 종속절 (시간 — before)

vocabulary.example: The agricultural revolution transformed human society.
Ancient civilizations built impressive structures.
The invention of the printing press gave rise to mass communication.

expressions.usage: 의미상 주어가 있는 목적절. for 뒤에 행위자가 옴
매우 먼 과거를 강조할 때
원인과 결과 관계를 표현하는 격식적 표현

patterns.pattern: In order for [someone] to [verb], [someone] would have to [verb] ... —maybe all the way back to [time], before [event]
patterns.explanation: 목적을 달성하기 위한 조건을 제시한 뒤, 대시로 구체적 추정을 부연하는 복합 패턴.
examples: In order for us to reach that level, we'd have to go back decades—maybe all the way back to the 1950s, before the digital revolution.

drillQuestions.question: In order _____ the 1750 guy to have as much fun, he'd have to go farther back.
The Agricultural Revolution gave _____ to the first cities.

sentences.notes: "as much fun as we had with him" — 이 비교 구문에서 "fun"이 "충격으로 죽게 만드는 재미"를 의미하는 것이 블랙 유머다. "give rise to"는 격식적 표현으로, 이 캐주얼한 글 안에서 "Agricultural Revolution"이라는 학술적 개념을 소개할 때 사용.

koreanAlignment.en: No
in order for the 1750 guy to have as much fun as we had with him
he'd have to go much farther back
koreanAlignment.ko: 훨씬 더 먼 과거로 가야 한다

koreanAlignment.en: maybe all the way back to about 12,000 BC
before the First Agricultural Revolution gave rise to the first cities
koreanAlignment.ko: 최초의 농업 혁명이 최초의 도시를 탄생시키기 전으로

koreanAlignment.en: and to the concept of civilization

sentences.original: If someone from a purely hunter-gatherer world—from a time when humans were, more or less, just another animal species—saw the vast human empires of 1750 with their towering churches, their ocean-crossing ships, their concept of being "inside," and their enormous mountain of collective, accumulated human knowledge and discovery—he'd likely die.
sentences.translation: 순수한 수렵-채집 세계에서 온 누군가가—인간이 대체로 그저 하나의 동물 종에 불과했던 시대에서 온—우뚝 솟은 교회, 대양을 건너는 배, "실내"라는 개념, 그리고 집단적으로 축적된 인간의 방대한 지식과 발견의 산이 있는 1750년의 거대한 제국을 본다면—아마 죽을 것이다.

grammarElements.text: If someone from a purely hunter-gatherer world
grammarElements.label: 조건절 주어

grammarElements.text: from a time when humans were, more or less, just another animal species
grammarElements.label: 대시 삽입구 (부연 설명)

grammarElements.text: saw the vast human empires of 1750
grammarElements.label: 조건절 동사 (가정법 과거)

grammarElements.text: with their towering churches, their ocean-crossing ships, their concept of being "inside," and their enormous mountain of collective, accumulated human knowledge and discovery
grammarElements.label: 전치사구 (with — 1750년 묘사)

vocabulary.example: Hunter-gatherer societies lived in small groups.
The towering skyscrapers dominated the skyline.
This represents our collective knowledge.
The accumulated evidence was overwhelming.

expressions.usage: 정확하지 않지만 대략적으로 맞는 것을 표현
앞서 확립한 "die" 척도를 다시 적용

patterns.pattern: If someone from [world A]—from a time when [description]—saw [world B] with their [X], [Y], [Z]—he'd likely [verb].
patterns.explanation: 두 세계를 극적으로 대비하고 결과를 제시하는 복합 조건문. 대시가 삽입 설명과 극적 결론을 모두 담당.
examples: If someone from ancient Egypt—from a time when pyramids were the pinnacle of technology—saw modern cities with their skyscrapers, airports, and satellite dishes—he'd likely faint.

drillQuestions.question: Humans were, more or less, just another animal _____.
He saw the enormous mountain of collective, _____ human knowledge.

sentences.notes: 이 문장은 4개의 "their"로 시작하는 병렬 나열(towering churches, ocean-crossing ships, concept of being "inside", enormous mountain of knowledge)이 1750년 문명의 위엄을 쌓아올린다. "their concept of being inside"는 건축물이 없던 시대 사람에게 "실내"라는 개념 자체가 놀라울 것이라는 통찰.

koreanAlignment.en: If someone from a purely hunter-gatherer world
from a time when humans were, more or less, just another animal species
koreanAlignment.ko: 인간이 대체로 그저 하나의 동물 종에 불과했던 시대에서

koreanAlignment.en: saw the vast human empires of 1750
with their towering churches
their ocean-crossing ships
their concept of being "inside"
and their enormous mountain of collective, accumulated human knowledge
koreanAlignment.ko: 집단적으로 축적된 인간 지식의 방대한 산

koreanAlignment.en: he'd likely die

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 열거법(enumeration) — 네 가지 "their" 항목을 쌓아올려 문명의 장대함을 축적적으로 표현

sentences.original: In order for someone to be transported into the future and die from the level of shock they'd experience, they have to go enough years ahead that a "die level of progress," or a Die Progress Unit (DPU) has been achieved.
sentences.translation: 누군가가 미래로 이동하여 경험하는 충격의 수준으로 죽으려면, 충분한 연수를 앞으로 가서 "죽을 수준의 진보", 즉 죽음 진보 단위(DPU)가 달성되어야 한다.

grammarElements.text: In order for someone to be transported into the future
grammarElements.label: 목적절 (in order for + 의미상 주어 + to부정사)

grammarElements.text: and die from the level of shock they'd experience
grammarElements.label: 등위 목적절

grammarElements.text: they have to go enough years ahead
grammarElements.label: 주절

grammarElements.text: that a "die level of progress," or a Die Progress Unit (DPU) has been achieved
grammarElements.label: 결과절 (enough ... that)

vocabulary.example: The goods were transported by ship.
The goal has been achieved.
Humanity has made incredible progress.

expressions.phrase: die from the level of shock
expressions.usage: 극도의 놀라움을 "죽음"으로 수량화하는 유머러스한 표현
저자가 만든 조어(neologism)로, 충격적 진보의 양을 측정하는 허구적 단위

patterns.pattern: In order for [someone] to [verb], they have to [verb] enough [noun] that [result clause]
patterns.explanation: 목적 달성을 위한 조건과 그 결과를 enough...that으로 연결하는 패턴.
examples: In order for students to pass, they have to study enough hours that the material is fully understood.
In order for the experiment to succeed, we have to gather enough data that meaningful conclusions can be drawn.

drillQuestions.question: They have to go enough years ahead _____ a DPU has been achieved.
A Die Progress Unit has been _____.

sentences.notes: "Die Progress Unit"는 Tim Urban이 만든 조어(neologism)로, 복잡한 개념을 유머러스하고 기억하기 쉬운 용어로 포장하는 그의 글쓰기 특징이 잘 드러난다. "die"라는 극단적 단어를 학술적 용어처럼 포장한 것이 핵심.

koreanAlignment.en: In order for someone to be transported into the future
and die from the level of shock they'd experience
they have to go enough years ahead
that a "die level of progress"
or a Die Progress Unit (DPU) has been achieved

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 조어법(neologism) — "Die Progress Unit"라는 새로운 용어를 만들어 복잡한 개념을 직관적이고 유머러스하게 전달

sentences.original: So a DPU took over 100,000 years in hunter-gatherer times, but at the post-Agricultural Revolution rate, it only took about 12,000 years.
sentences.translation: 수렵-채집 시대에는 DPU가 10만 년 이상 걸렸지만, 농업 혁명 이후의 속도에서는 약 12,000년밖에 걸리지 않았다.

grammarElements.text: but at the post-Agricultural Revolution rate
grammarElements.label: 대조 접속사 + 전치사구

vocabulary.example: The post-war economy grew rapidly.
The rate of change has been accelerating.

expressions.usage: 기간이 특정 숫자를 초과했음을 표현
only로 기간이 짧아졌음을 강조

patterns.pattern: [X] took [long time] in [period A], but at the [period B] rate, it only took [short time]
patterns.explanation: 두 시대의 속도를 수치로 직접 비교하여 가속을 논증하는 패턴.
examples: Crossing the Atlantic took months in the Age of Sail, but at modern aviation rates, it only takes hours.
Sending a message took weeks in the postal era, but at the internet rate, it only takes seconds.

drillQuestions.question: A DPU took _____ 100,000 years in hunter-gatherer times.
At the post-Agricultural Revolution rate, it _____ took about 12,000 years.

sentences.notes: 100,000년 vs 12,000년의 구체적 수치 비교가 가속의 개념을 즉각적으로 체감시킨다. "post-"는 접두사로 "~이후의"를 의미하며, post-Agricultural, post-Industrial처럼 역사적 시대 구분에 자주 사용.

koreanAlignment.en: a DPU took over 100,000 years
in hunter-gatherer times
but at the post-Agricultural Revolution rate
it only took about 12,000 years

sentences.original: The post-Industrial Revolution world has moved so quickly that a 1750 person only needs to go forward a couple hundred years for a DPU to have happened.
sentences.translation: 산업 혁명 이후의 세계는 너무나 빠르게 움직여서, 1750년 사람은 DPU가 발생하려면 겨우 수백 년만 앞으로 가면 된다.

grammarElements.text: that a 1750 person only needs to go forward a couple hundred years
grammarElements.label: 결과절 (so ... that)

grammarElements.text: for a DPU to have happened
grammarElements.label: 목적절 (for + 의미상 주어 + 완료 to부정사)

vocabulary.example: The post-Industrial Revolution era brought unprecedented change.
It happened a couple hundred years ago.

expressions.usage: so...that 결과 구문으로 속도와 그 결과를 연결

expressions.phrase: for a DPU to have happened
expressions.usage: for + 의미상 주어 + 완료 to부정사로 조건을 표현

patterns.pattern: [Subject] has moved so quickly that [someone] only needs to [verb] [short amount] for [X] to have happened
patterns.explanation: so...that 구문으로 가속의 결과를 수치화하여 보여주는 패턴.
examples: Technology has moved so quickly that a 2000 person only needs to go forward 20 years for a major paradigm shift to have happened.

drillQuestions.question: The world has moved _____ quickly that major changes happen in decades.
A 1750 person only _____ to go forward a couple hundred years.

sentences.notes: so...that 결과 구문이 사용되었다. "for a DPU to have happened"에서 완료 to부정사(to have happened)는 미래의 어느 시점에서 이미 완료된 상태를 나타낸다. 100,000년 → 12,000년 → 수백 년으로 가속이 점점 빨라지는 패턴의 완성.

koreanAlignment.en: The post-Industrial Revolution world
has moved so quickly
that a 1750 person only needs to go forward
a couple hundred years
for a DPU to have happened

pronunciationNotes: couple: /ˈkʌpl/ — "커플", 미국식에서 "a couple hundred"는 약 200을 비격식적으로 표현

sentences.original: This pattern—human progress moving quicker and quicker as time goes on—is what futurist Ray Kurzweil calls human history's Law of Accelerating Returns.
sentences.translation: 이 패턴—시간이 지날수록 인류의 진보가 점점 더 빨라지는 것—은 미래학자 레이 커즈와일이 인류 역사의 수확 가속의 법칙이라 부르는 것이다.

grammarElements.text: human progress moving quicker and quicker as time goes on
grammarElements.label: 대시 삽입구 (동격 — pattern의 내용)

grammarElements.text: what futurist Ray Kurzweil calls human history's Law of Accelerating Returns
grammarElements.label: 보어 (what 관계대명사절)

vocabulary.example: The futurist predicted dramatic changes in the next decade.
The accelerating pace of change is difficult to comprehend.
The investment yielded high returns.

expressions.usage: 비교급 반복으로 점진적 가속을 표현
시간의 경과에 따른 변화를 나타내는 관용적 표현
Kurzweil이 명명한 기술 발전 가속 이론의 공식 명칭

patterns.pattern: This pattern—[description]—is what [expert] calls [concept name]
patterns.explanation: 앞에서 설명한 현상에 공식 명칭을 부여하고 권위자를 인용하는 학술적 서술 패턴.
examples: This pattern—the rich getting richer—is what economists call the Matthew effect.
This pattern—species evolving rapidly after a mass extinction—is what biologists call adaptive radiation.

drillQuestions.question: This pattern is _____ futurist Ray Kurzweil calls the Law of Accelerating Returns.
Human progress is moving quicker and _____ as time goes on.

sentences.notes: 대시 사이에 삽입된 동격구가 "this pattern"의 내용을 구체적으로 설명한다. "what X calls Y"는 what 관계대명사절로 "X가 Y라고 부르는 것"이라는 의미. quicker and quicker는 비교급 반복 패턴으로 가속을 언어적으로도 체현.

koreanAlignment.en: This pattern
human progress moving quicker and quicker as time goes on
koreanAlignment.ko: 시간이 지날수록 인류의 진보가 점점 더 빨라지는 것

koreanAlignment.en: is what futurist Ray Kurzweil calls
human history's Law of Accelerating Returns

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 명명(naming) — 앞서 관찰한 패턴에 학술적 이름을 부여하여 개념을 공식화하고 기억에 남김

sentences.original: This happens because more advanced societies have the ability to progress at a faster rate than less advanced societies—because they're more advanced.
sentences.translation: 이것은 더 발전한 사회가 덜 발전한 사회보다 더 빠른 속도로 진보할 수 있는 능력을 가지기 때문이다—그들이 더 발전했기 때문에.

grammarElements.text: because more advanced societies have the ability to progress at a faster rate than less advanced societies
grammarElements.label: 이유절 (because)

vocabulary.example: Japan is a technologically advanced nation.
Humans have the ability to learn complex languages.
The economy is growing at a fast rate.

expressions.usage: 속도의 비교를 나타내는 전치사구
순환 논리처럼 보이지만 자기 강화적 과정을 설명하는 표현

patterns.pattern: This happens because [X] [verb] at a faster rate than [Y]—because they're more [adjective].
patterns.explanation: 동어반복(tautology)적 구조로 자기 강화적 메커니즘(positive feedback loop)을 설명하는 패턴.
examples: This happens because wealthy people earn more than poor people—because they're wealthier.
This happens because experienced workers learn faster than beginners—because they're more experienced.

drillQuestions.question: More advanced societies progress at a _____ rate than less advanced ones.
This happens because they're more _____ .

sentences.notes: 대시 뒤의 "because they're more advanced"는 의도적인 동어반복(tautology). 순환 논리처럼 보이지만, 이것이 바로 가속의 본질—발전이 더 빠른 발전을 낳는 양성 피드백 루프(positive feedback loop)—을 설명하는 것이다. Tim Urban은 이 순환성을 의도적으로 부각시킨다.

koreanAlignment.en: This happens because
more advanced societies have the ability to progress
koreanAlignment.ko: 더 발전한 사회가 진보할 수 있는 능력을 가지기

koreanAlignment.en: at a faster rate than less advanced societies
koreanAlignment.ko: 덜 발전한 사회보다 더 빠른 속도로

koreanAlignment.en: because they're more advanced

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 동어반복(tautology) — 의도적으로 순환적 설명을 사용하여 양성 피드백 루프의 본질을 드러냄

sentences.original: So—advances are getting bigger and bigger and happening more and more quickly. This suggests some pretty intense things about our future, right?
sentences.translation: 그러니까—발전은 점점 더 커지고 점점 더 빠르게 일어나고 있다. 이것은 우리 미래에 대해 꽤 강렬한 것들을 시사하지 않는가?

grammarElements.text: and happening more and more quickly
grammarElements.label: 병렬 동사 (현재진행 + 비교급 반복)

grammarElements.text: some pretty intense things about our future
grammarElements.label: 목적어

vocabulary.example: The competition was intense.
The data suggests a new trend.

expressions.usage: 비교급 반복으로 점진적 증가를 표현
부사의 비교급 반복으로 가속을 표현
pretty가 "상당히"라는 의미의 강조 부사로 사용

patterns.pattern: [Subject] are getting [comparative] and [comparative] and happening more and more [adverb]. This suggests [something], right?
patterns.explanation: 비교급 반복으로 가속 트렌드를 묘사한 뒤, 독자에게 직접 질문하여 참여를 유도하는 패턴.
examples: Temperatures are getting higher and higher and disasters are happening more and more frequently. This suggests something alarming, right?

drillQuestions.question: Advances are getting _____ and bigger.
This _____ some pretty intense things about our future.

sentences.notes: 비교급 반복 패턴("bigger and bigger", "more and more quickly")이 글의 핵심 주장—가속—을 언어적으로 체현한다. 마지막의 "right?"는 독자에게 직접 말을 건네는 Tim Urban의 대화체 스타일.

koreanAlignment.en: advances are getting bigger and bigger
and happening more and more quickly
This suggests some pretty intense things
about our future, right?

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 수사적 질문(rhetorical question) — "right?"로 독자의 동의를 유도하며 결론으로 이끎
